elements.sent-2411-135: While partial automation may be a relevant metric to track, many of the most severe risks under consideration by AI developers and others---such as explosive feedback loops, ``self-improvement'' and sabotage---become particularly acute when systems can fully automate R\&D tasks over long time horizons with minimal human oversight.
elements.sent-2411-136: Full automation is also conceptually simpler to evaluate, since demonstrating any critical missing capability is sufficient to conclude that it is unlikely.
elements.sent-2411-137: Managing these risks effectively may require developers to implement a wide range of mitigations (such as improved accountability mechanisms, and increased investment in control and alignment techniques).
elements.sent-2411-138: Securing the model weights has been identified as a mitigation of particular importance, which may be challenging to implement\cite{openai2023preparedness,deepmind2024frontier,Nevo2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-139: Security-related mitigations will often need to be implemented before an AI R\&D capable model is created, since model weight theft could occur during or shortly after training.
elements.sent-2411-140: As a result, AI developers have identified the need for early warning evaluations, which can provide advance assurance that some further training or elicitation (within a ‘safety buffer’) will not lead to significant AI R\&D risks\cite{anthropic2024rsp,deepmind2024frontier .}
elements.sent-2411-141: Effective early warning evaluations need to have a low risk of underestimating AI capabilities, while being practical and avoiding early saturation.
elements.sent-2411-142: Some key challenges of achieving this include:.
elements.sent-2411-143: Feasibility: Achieving a high score on the evaluation has to be feasible without demanding extreme levels of capability or guesswork.
elements.sent-2411-144: To be solvable, an evaluation needs to (among other things) provide unambiguous instructions, avoid blocking technical issues, and provide enough time and resources.\footnote{Some evaluations might deliberately want to test the ability to interpret ambiguous instructions.}
elements.sent-2411-145: In this case significant care may be needed to ensure and demonstrate that the correct interpretation can be determined with sufficient skill and effort.
elements.sent-2411-146: This has come up as a frequent issue with many complex evaluations; for instance, many SWE-bench~\cite{jimenez2023swebench problems turned out to be impossible and under-specified on closer inspection.}
elements.sent-2411-147: This continued to hold true even after an attempt was made to identify a verified-to-be-feasible subset~\cite{anthropic2024swebench .}
elements.sent-2411-148: Ecological validity: To be able to calibrate risk thresholds without adding unnecessary margin or taking on excessive risk, there needs to be a clear link between evaluation results and risk levels.
elements.sent-2411-150: For example, it is unclear how scores in MLE-bench translate into the ability of AI agents to automate AI R\&D.
elements.sent-2411-151: Resistance to saturation: In recent years, most AI benchmarks and evaluations have been saturating rapidly~\cite{maslej2024artificialintelligenceindexreport .}
elements.sent-2411-152: If early warning evaluations saturate long before AI agents pose significant risks, they can no longer serve as an early warning.
elements.sent-2411-153: There are many potential causes of this, including the risk of contamination and the limited scope of many evaluations, and we discuss some approaches to mitigate this in Section \ref{sec:benchmark .}
elements.sent-2411-154: We think that more emphasis on faithful human comparisons can help significantly with the first two challenges (Section \ref{sec:benchmark ).}
elements.sent-2411-155: By comparing to humans under equivalent conditions, we can confirm that our evaluations are feasible and provide crucial grounding for interpreting agent results.

elements.sent-2411-167: To meet the three challenges to robust and effective early warning evaluations identified in Section \ref{subsec:early_warning ---feasibility, ecological validity, and resistance to saturation---we set out to create a benchmark that directly compared human experts to AI agents in realistic AI R\&D environments.}
elements.sent-2411-168: To enable us to iterate quickly and gather data at a reasonable cost, we decided on two practical constraints: we evaluate human experts over 8 hours, and make sure all environments can be run with 8 or fewer H100 GPUs.
elements.sent-2411-169: The environments were designed with two key pillars in mind: (i) maximize coverage of key frontier AI R\&D challenges, while (ii) ensuring that human experts under the same conditions as the agents can reliably make steady progress on the task, without running into issues or hitting a score ceiling.
elements.sent-2411-170: Covering a large variety of the challenges involved in AI R\&D reduces the risk of early saturation if AI agents become capable of some but not all of the key sub-skills needed to automate AI R\&D.
elements.sent-2411-171: Similarly, ensuring that environments have a high ceiling, beyond what humans can achieve within the time budget, reduces the risk that the evaluation saturates due to both agents and humans reaching the top score.
elements.sent-2411-172: Meanwhile, ensuring that human experts reliably make progress provides assurance that the environments are feasible and have unambiguous instructions.
elements.sent-2411-173: Together these two pillars can allow for significantly improved grounding, by observing that if agents do worse than humans at key frontier AI R\&D activities for the same cost, then they are unlikely to cost-effectively automate these experts.
elements.sent-2411-174: This does not follow in the other direction (i.e. agents might do better than human experts on this evaluation, without being capable of automating AI R\&D in practice), since these environments are very far from covering all the challenges involved in frontier AI R\&D (which generally occurs on a timescale of months or years, rather than a single day).
elements.sent-2411-175: We will first describe the seven environments in the benchmark, then discuss how we gathered human baselines and finally go into detail on how we approached and assessed these requirements.

elements.sent-2411-176: The current \EvalName{ suite includes seven hand-crafted novel evaluation environments (see Table \ref{table:summary ).}}
elements.sent-2411-177: Each environment presents a unique ML optimization problem, where achieving a high score generally requires significant experimentation, implementation, and efficient use of compute resources.
elements.sent-2411-178: We designed these environments to have a high ceiling, but still allow significant progress with just 8 hours of time and limited hardware resources (at most 6 H100s).
elements.sent-2411-180: A scoring function, which defines the goal of the environment, and can be run by the agent at any time.
elements.sent-2411-181: Each time the scoring function is run a timestamped entry is added to a score log, allowing us to reconstruct progress over time.
elements.sent-2411-182: In almost all environments, the agent can see its score log, and inspect the details of the scoring function, to help it understand its goal.\footnote{Allowing the agent to check the score of its submissions in `Scaling Law Experiment' would remove the need to extrapolate from small-scale experiments, and thus undermine the point of the environment.}
elements.sent-2411-183: A (simple but poorly performing) starting solution which is provided to the agent and demonstrates what a valid solution looks like.
elements.sent-2411-184: This helps clarify the environment setup and allows the agent to get to the challenging research components of the evaluation faster.
elements.sent-2411-185: For example, in `Optimize a Kernel,' the agent is provided with a simple but slow Python solution.
elements.sent-2411-186: A reference solution, created by the task author, which scores highly.
elements.sent-2411-187: This solution is not provided to the agent, but is used in normalizing scores, and serves as an example of a good solution for researchers.
elements.sent-2411-188: For all environments except `Scaling Law Experiment' (where only the final submission counts), the agent is assessed by the highest score registered in the score log at the end of the run.
elements.sent-2411-189: Since each evaluation environment defines a different scoring metric, we use a normalization scheme to make scores comparable between them.
elements.sent-2411-190: For each environment we linearly transform the raw score $y$ to a normalized score $y_n$ so that the starting score $y_s$ (achieved by running the scoring function on the starting solution) is $0$, and the reference solution score $y_r$ (achieved by running the scoring function on the reference solution) is 1: \begin{equation}y_n = \frac{y-y_s}{y_r-y_s}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-191: It is possible to achieve scores significantly exceeding the reference solution, though we have not observed any normalized scores above $2$.
elements.sent-2411-192: In addition, we assign solutions that score worse than the starting solution a score of 0.
elements.sent-2411-193: Since the difficulty of matching the reference solution varies by task, the average normalized score achieved also varies (in experiments with human experts over 8 hours, the average score is between $0.5$--$1.5$ for each environment).
elements.sent-2411-194: We evaluated environments at 3 stages of development.
elements.sent-2411-195: Specification: Environment ideas were generated through discussions with ML practitioners, reading relevant literature or from past work experiences of the METR staff.
elements.sent-2411-196: To assess the idea, written descriptions of the proposed environments, scoring functions, and starting solutions were evaluated and reworked until we were optimistic that implementation would not be too challenging, and that the specification could do well on our two design pillars.
elements.sent-2411-197: Implementation: An initial full implementation of the evaluation was created, along with an internal solution.
elements.sent-2411-198: These were again evaluated and reworked until we were optimistic about the implementation meeting our requirements.
elements.sent-2411-199: Baselining: After trials with human experts, we would evaluate how the environment performed on our criteria, and often make significant adjustments.
elements.sent-2411-200: Each environment is the product of significant iteration, and in the process of developing these 7, we dropped over 12 specifications (at various levels of completion), and more than 5 completed implementations which encountered unexpected difficulties or performed poorly on our criteria.
elements.subsection-2411-013: Coverage of key AI R\&D challenges
elements.sent-2411-201: Achieving good coverage of the many challenges involved in advancing AI R\&D while ensuring experts can make significant progress in just 8 hours is challenging, since real AI R\&D research projects usually take months to complete.
elements.sent-2411-202: This creates a trade-off between different types of difficulty (e.g. engineering complexity, novelty, slow feedback loops), where human experts or AI agents have to split their time between different kinds of difficulty.
elements.sent-2411-203: For instance, an environment that has significant engineering complexity may not leave as much time to come up with and test novel ideas, and thus will not emphasize those skills as much.

elements.subsection-2411-021: Characteristics of environments with small human--AI gap
elements.sent-2411-290: Based on these observations, we might expect that AI agents would do especially well compared to humans in environments with:.
elements.sent-2411-291: Short and high-fidelity feedback loops, which allow AI agents to lean into their ability to rapidly try many solutions.
elements.sent-2411-292: Low engineering complexity, so that AI agents can solve the environments in a few steps rather than needing to build complex programs over longer time horizons.
elements.sent-2411-293: Specialized expertise requirements, which AI agents are more likely to have than most human experts.
elements.sent-2411-294: Significant noisiness which benefits the many attempts AI agent make over the fewer human expert attempts.
elements.sent-2411-295: Few surprises and little need for exploration or discovery, which AI agents may struggle with.

elements.sent-2411-296: It is difficult to validate these empirically with so few environments, but in Figure \ref{fig:environment_complexity we find that our results are compatible with low engineering complexity contributing to a smaller human--AI gap ($R^2=0.602$), while we did not find a clear connection with length of feedback loops or novelty.}

elements.sent-2411-298: We expect the human--AI gap in real-world AI R\&D to be much larger than the gap observed on these evaluations, and find it fairly plausible that the first agents that match top human performance in these environments may still be far from capable of AI R\&D automation.
elements.sent-2411-299: Specifically, we believe there are two main reasons why an AI agent might perform well on our benchmark yet fail to contribute meaningfully to the automation of AI R\&D: Limited scope of tasks.
elements.sent-2411-300: The scale and complexity of real world AI R\&D is at least 2 orders of magnitude (OOM) larger across many dimensions than those encountered in our environments (Table \ref{table:real-transfer ).}
elements.sent-2411-301: We have seen that the human--AI gap increases over longer time horizons and qualitatively found indications that the same is true of high engineering complexity.
elements.sent-2411-303: Our tasks are well defined (in that each involves writing a modest amount of code to optimize a specified objective) and do not involve coordination between many interacting workstreams.
elements.sent-2411-304: We have not been able to investigate how agents deal with orchestrating parallel projects or with resolving ambiguous instructions, but given their difficulty identifying and fixing simple issues with their own environment, they may struggle with the additional communication bottlenecks, constraints and coordination difficulties required.
elements.sent-2411-305: We also observe anecdotally that current AI agents seem to be much further from autonomously automating AI R\&D than the relatively modest gap we observe in our environments would imply.
elements.sent-2411-306: Agents are substantially cheaper than human experts: On average, our agents use \textasciitilde{ 29M input tokens and \textasciitilde{ 499K output tokens in each 8-hour run, at a cost of approximately \$123.}}
elements.sent-2411-307: This is only a small fraction of the approximately \$1,855 that we paid our human experts on average (and what a skilled human researcher costs in general), and the cost of agent runs could potentially be much lower if proper prompt caching was used.

elements.sent-2411-308: Even if an AI agent takes longer time (or many more attempts) than a human expert to accomplish AI R\&D tasks, they might nonetheless end up economically competitive with human researchers due to lower costs.
elements.sent-2411-310: The tasks in \EvalName{ are designed using the AI R\&D workflows of human researchers.}
elements.sent-2411-311: It may be possible that AI agents can automate AI research using alternative workflows that do not require performing similar tasks; for example, AI agents are capable of generating synthetic data, and future workflows may involve more such data generation.
elements.sent-2411-312: Similarly, these evaluations require using existing standard software packages or interfaces optimized for human usage; it is possible that future AI research will involve more specialized tooling on which AI agents outperform humans.
elements.sent-2411-313: There are also other limitations to our results and benchmark, which we hope to address in future work.
elements.sent-2411-314: Evaluation design criteria lead to unrepresentative environments: In order to create high-reliability evaluations matching our design criteria we have tried to ensure that instructions and scoring are easily understood, that significant progress is possible within 8 hours, and that all necessary resources are provided.
elements.sent-2411-315: We have also had to select for environments that are easy for us to construct and assess.
elements.sent-2411-316: These constraints make the evaluation environments less representative of real research, where unclear goals, poor instructions, slow feedback and impossible problems are common.
elements.sent-2411-317: Noisy agent results due to rare successes: Our results have significant noise from the small number of environments.
elements.sent-2411-318: Since agent scores are heavily skewed toward the right, with most runs scoring 0 and a few achieving very high scores, our estimates are sensitive to sampling noise.
elements.sent-2411-319: Cost and complexity of evaluation: Running agents for many hours with access to H100 GPUs requires significant infrastructure and a large budget.
elements.sent-2411-320: This makes the evaluation less accessible to researchers, and makes it more challenging to run large-scale experiments comparing many models, scaffoldings and parameters.
elements.sent-2411-321: Lack of scaffold iteration: Different agent scaffolds or prompts might be able to achieve a much better score on our benchmark in a similar time.
elements.sent-2411-322: In particular, we expect better performance from giving the agents better tools for managing GPU resources, and from approaches that make use of a larger number of tokens, e.g., by exploring many solutions in parallel (though limited compute resources pose limitations for this approach).
elements.sent-2411-323: Limitations in covering frontier research: Due to the limited hardware access, and because frontier AI research is increasingly siloed within large AI developers, there may be significant differences between the kinds of research these evaluations cover, and the kinds driving frontier AI progress.
elements.sent-2411-324: Agent solutions may be overfitted: All environments except for ``Scaling Law Experiment’’ provide the agents with the test score output (to minimize the risk of misunderstandings or confusions).
elements.sent-2411-325: In experiments with humans, this did not seem to allow for significant overfitting as long as datasets were withheld, but AI agents are able to score many more solutions.
elements.sent-2411-326: In future iterations, we will likely only provide agents with a validation score in most environments, keeping the test score hidden.
elements.sent-2411-327: This issue seems to especially affect ``Finetune GPT-2 for QA’’, which likely underestimates the human--AI gap in this environment.
elements.sent-2411-328: A better version of this environment would ensure that random guessing did much worse than real solutions.
elements.sent-2411-329: In this work, we presented \EvalName{ , a suite of environments that measure the ability of AI agents to automate AI R\&D tasks.}
elements.sent-2411-330: From our human experts baselines, we believe that achieving a good score on \EvalName{ is feasible---though there is significant variance in the results.}
elements.sent-2411-331: We also find that the environments are challenging, and most are not saturated even by the top human experts in 8 hours.
elements.sent-2411-332: We hope that these properties will allow for useful direct comparisons between human expert performance and AI agent performance on (short and self-contained) AI R\&D activities.
elements.sent-2411-333: Evaluating some current frontier AI agents we find that, when evaluated through best-of-$k$ with 8 hours of total compute budget, they achieve scores close to the average human expert, demonstrating very impressive capabilities.
elements.sent-2411-334: However a significant gap remains compared to the top human performance in most environments, as seen in Figure \ref{fig:bar_by_env .}
elements.sent-2411-335: Monitoring whether and how quickly AI agents are bridging this gap may help predict the emergence of autonomous AI R\&D automation.
elements.sent-2411-336: However, as discussed above, there are some significant limitations to this work.
elements.sent-2411-337: Future work may want to focus on expanding these evaluations to focus on longer time horizons, more expensive or challenging feedback loops and greater engineering complexity.

elements.sent-2411-339: For human expert runs, we did the following:.
elements.sent-2411-340: We had human participants SSH into a VM set up through Vivaria~\cite{vivaria2024 .}
elements.sent-2411-341: For practical reasons, each human run was limited to 8 hours.
elements.sent-2411-342: We gave participants some context on what we were trying to accomplish, and how to approach the environments.
elements.sent-2411-343: In particular we encourage participants to be ``greedy,'' optimizing for improving their score within the first 2 hours, and then taking an iterative approach from there.
elements.sent-2411-344: We find that this encouragement helps avoid overambitious attempts that do not reach a score in time.
elements.sent-2411-345: Participants were allowed to browse the internet and use LLMs and other tools in solving the problem.
elements.sent-2411-346: Participants were instructed to make frequent commits to a GitHub repository, which we could inspect to check their progress at different points during the run.
elements.sent-2411-347: Participants were encouraged to run the scoring function frequently (which would be recorded in a score log).
elements.sent-2411-348: Participants were encouraged to write timestamped research logs and reach out about about any technical issues or ambiguities during the run.
elements.sent-2411-349: Participants were allowed to take breaks by reporting their start and stop times.
elements.sent-2411-350: We did not carefully monitor whether people spent exactly 8 hours in all cases, though it is unlikely that there are very significant discrepancies, since commit logs roughly match stated start/stop points, and we expect most participants were honest.

elements.sent-2411-359: We used two different scaffolds, the \href{https://github.com/poking-agents/modular-public {Modular agent , a general-purpose agent scaffold which METR has used for general autonomy tasks before, and a lightly adapted version of \href{https://github.com/WecoAI/aideml {AIDE .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-360: Modular: This scaffold provides the model with Python and Bash commands, and some very simple context management to keep the input within the context window length of the LM.
elements.sent-2411-361: The same agent was used across the suite---there was no environment-specific prompting or scaffolding.
elements.sent-2411-362: The simple agent scaffolding worked as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-363: We provided a system prompt that describes the agent setup and the tools available to the model.
elements.sent-2411-364: The environment instructions are given in the the first ``user'' message.
elements.sent-2411-365: The model has access to python and bash commands.
elements.sent-2411-366: The Claude agent uses XML tags in the assistant message to call commands.
elements.sent-2411-367: Each command is appended to the context as an Assistant message, and the resulting output is appended as a User message.
elements.sent-2411-368: All command outputs get saved to local files.
elements.sent-2411-369: If the prompt is too long, a number of the earliest messages are replaced by a notice that the context has been truncated.
elements.sent-2411-370: The agent is provided with information about how much time has been used so far, as well as its remaining time budget.
elements.sent-2411-371: We made two specific changes to handle the fact that AI R\&D environments are harder and much more compute-intensive:.
elements.sent-2411-372: As the environments often require waiting long periods for training code to run, we allowed the model to modify the timeouts on its bash and python commands.
elements.sent-2411-373: As many of the environments have parallel components (e.g., running training code while reading a paper or looking at experimental results), we added instructions to the prompt reminding the model that it could run code in the background.
elements.sent-2411-374: As we found that Claude~3.5 Sonnet would often forget about running background processes, we also added instructions prompting it to check for background memory usage.
elements.sent-2411-375: Despite these additions, agents rarely made use of running code in the background.
elements.sent-2411-376: AIDE: We made minimal modifications to AIDE in order to make it compatible with our environments:.
elements.sent-2411-377: We ask the model what python file to write solutions in at the beginning, then whenever the agent writes python code we copy it into that file.
elements.sent-2411-378: Before analyzing execution outputs, we run scoring and provide the scoring function output to the feedback model.
elements.sent-2411-379: We use one directory for both data and workspace, instead of generating a new workspace directory.
elements.sent-2411-380: We filter out some hidden or cache files from the directory preview and truncate it.
elements.sent-2411-381: We use majority vote to determine whether scoring metric should be maximized or minimized.
elements.sent-2411-382: We remove some of the machine learning utility packages that the open-source variant installs to save time and prevent compatibility issues.

elements.sent-2411-538: We compare humans to several public frontier models through best-of-$k$ with varying time budgets and agent designs, and find that the best AI agents achieve a score \AgentMultipleHumanScoreAtTwoHours{ higher than human experts when both are given a total time budget of 2 hours per environment.}

elements.sent-2411-539: From our human expert baselines, we believe that achieving a good score on \EvalName{ is feasible---though there is significant variance in the results.}
elements.sent-2411-540: We expect bridging these gaps while preserving feasibility and direct human comparisons to be a challenging and expensive development direction, but one that might be needed in the near future to avoid having to make compromises on the sensitivity and reliability of AI R\&D evaluations.
